text.title: SQL Query Analysis
text.summary: How to read EXPLAIN ANALYZE output and identify the plan nodes that cause slow queries.

rule.statement: Always run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT) before adding any index or rewriting any query.
rule.rationale: The planner's chosen plan often differs from developer expectations. Optimization without measurement produces indexes that are never used and rewrites that change nothing.

example.code: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT) SELECT o.id, o.total_amount, u.email FROM orders o JOIN users u ON u.id = o.user_id WHERE o.status = 'pending' AND o.created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100;

list.item: Seq Scan — full table scan; acceptable on small tables (<10k rows), problematic on large ones.
Index Scan — uses a B-tree index; good for selective queries returning a small fraction of rows.
Bitmap Index Scan — combines multiple indexes; used when selectivity is medium.
Nested Loop — for each outer row, probes inner; fast when outer set is small; catastrophic when outer is large.
Hash Join — builds hash table from smaller relation, probes with larger; efficient for larger datasets.
Sort — watch for disk sorts (Sort Method: external); add index or increase work_mem.

rule.statement: Investigate when actual rows differ from estimated rows by more than 10x — this signals stale statistics.
rule.rationale: The planner chooses join order and index use based on row estimates. Large discrepancies cause poor plan choices that cannot be fixed by indexes alone; run ANALYZE first.
